claim: The harness (the code wrapper around an LLM) can produce a 6x performance gap on the same benchmark compared to different harnesses on a fixed model, making harness design as critical as model weights.
actionable: Invest as much engineering effort into harness optimization (prompting, retrieval, memory, state management) as you would into model selection when building AI systems.

claim: Meta Harness is an automated system that uses a coding agent to propose, evaluate, and iteratively improve harnesses without human intervention, achieving better results than handwritten harnesses.
actionable: Use meta-harness patterns in your own AI system development: version your harness code, log evaluation scores and execution traces, and let an agent propose improvements based on failure modes.

claim: Compressed feedback (summarizing execution traces into a few thousand tokens) loses critical information needed to improve harnesses; instead, give agents file-system access to full execution history.
actionable: When building self-improving systems, store full execution traces in versioned directories rather than compressing them; let the agent use grep/cat to selectively retrieve what it needs.

claim: Retrieval-augmented harness optimization (letting the model decide what context to access adaptively) outperforms monolithic harnesses that pack all context upfront.
actionable: Design harnesses that expose tools for adaptive context retrieval (memory lookup, document search, code inspection) rather than providing fixed context at initialization.

claim: Meta Harness on text classification achieves a median score of 50 vs. 39.1 from baseline methods, and on Terminal Bench it scores 76.4 (Opus) and 37.6 (Haiku), outperforming handwritten harnesses.
actionable: Benchmark your current harness on standard eval sets (text classification, math reasoning, terminal tasks) before and after applying self-improvement loops to measure impact.

claim: Math problem solutions often reuse proof patterns, so retrieval-augmented reasoning improves math performance (4.7 point average gain) even when solving novel problems.
actionable: For math reasoning tasks, implement a harness that retrieves similar solved problems and proof structures before attempting new problems.

claim: Claude Opus 4.6 is the recommended model for harness optimization agents because it handles code inspection, file-system navigation, and diagnostic reasoning better than other models.
actionable: When building self-improving harnesses, use Claude Opus 4.6 as your proposer agent; it excels at reading harness code and identifying failure patterns.

claim: Self-improving software follows the 'bitter lesson': handwritten heuristics always lose to learned patterns, so all code should eventually have its own meta-harness for self-evolution.
actionable: Treat harness engineering as infrastructure: as models improve, allocate more compute to harness self-improvement loops than to manually tuning prompts or rules.
